items.title: CRUD lifecycle end to end (MVC controller to MSSQL)
items.description: A complete CRUD flow in ASP.NET Core MVC moves a request through controller action, model binding, validation, a service/repository layer, EF Core DbContext, and finally MSSQL. List (Index) and detail (Details) are read paths; Create/Edit are GET-renders-form then POST-persists; Delete is a POST confirmed by a GET preview to avoid destructive GETs. Keep the DbContext scoped per request (the default AddDbContext lifetime) and never share it across threads, because EF Core's change tracker is not thread-safe.
items.applicability: Applies to every entity-backed page in the platform: Projects, Knowledge, Models, and any new CRUD surface over the EF Core AppDbContext.
items.example: [HttpPost, ValidateAntiForgeryToken] public async Task<IActionResult> Create(ProjectEditVm vm){ if(!ModelState.IsValid) return View(vm); var e = _mapper.ToEntity(vm); _db.Projects.Add(e); await _db.SaveChangesAsync(); return RedirectToAction(nameof(Index)); }
items.limitation: The POST-Redirect-GET pattern is required so a browser refresh after Create/Edit does not resubmit the form; returning a View directly after a successful POST reintroduces double-submit bugs.

items.title: Model binding and dedicated view models
items.description: Bind POST forms to dedicated view models (DTOs), never directly to EF entities, to prevent over-posting / mass-assignment where a hidden or crafted field mutates a property the user must not control (e.g. IsApproved, OwnerId, CreatedBy). Map view model to entity explicitly in the service layer so the set of writable properties is auditable in one place. Use [Bind] sparingly; an explicit view model is clearer and survives refactors better than attribute whitelisting.
items.applicability: All Create/Edit POST actions across the platform that accept user input and persist to MSSQL.
items.example:
// Vulnerable: public IActionResult Edit(KnowledgeItem item) -- user can post IsApproved=true.
// Safe: public IActionResult Edit(KnowledgeEditVm vm) then map only Title/Content/Tags onto the loaded entity.
items.limitation: Hand-mapping adds boilerplate; a mapper (AutoMapper/manual) reduces it but you must still exclude security-sensitive fields from the inbound view model entirely, not just from the mapping.

items.title: Validation in CRUD forms (client and server)
items.description: Validate on the server as the source of truth via DataAnnotations and/or IValidatableObject, and surface errors through ModelState; client-side unobtrusive validation is a UX convenience only and must never be trusted. Re-render the same view with the posted view model when ModelState is invalid so the user keeps their input and sees field-level messages. Cross-field and database-dependent rules (uniqueness, referential existence) belong in the server action or service, not in attributes alone.
items.applicability: Every form POST; especially uniqueness checks (e.g. duplicate project name) that require a database round-trip.
items.example: if(await _db.Projects.AnyAsync(p=>p.Name==vm.Name && p.Id!=vm.Id)) ModelState.AddModelError(nameof(vm.Name),"Name already in use"); if(!ModelState.IsValid) return View(vm);
items.limitation: Uniqueness checked in code has a TOCTOU race; back it with a UNIQUE index in MSSQL and catch DbUpdateException to convert the constraint violation into a friendly ModelState error.

items.title: Transaction boundary placement
items.description: A single SaveChangesAsync is already atomic — EF Core wraps all pending changes in one implicit transaction — so do not add an explicit transaction around one save. Use an explicit transaction (db.Database.BeginTransactionAsync) only when you must coordinate multiple SaveChanges calls, raw SQL, or an external side effect inside one unit of work. Keep transactions short: open late, commit early, never await user input or a network/HTTP call while a transaction (and its locks) is held, or you will cause MSSQL lock contention and blocking.
items.applicability: Multi-step writes such as import staging promotion, approval workflows that touch several tables, or write-then-enqueue patterns.
items.example: await using var tx = await _db.Database.BeginTransactionAsync(); _db.Add(parent); await _db.SaveChangesAsync(); child.ParentId = parent.Id; _db.Add(child); await _db.SaveChangesAsync(); await tx.CommitAsync();
items.limitation: Execution strategies (connection resiliency / retry-on-failure) forbid an ambient user transaction unless wrapped in strategy.ExecuteAsync; otherwise a transient retry would replay only part of the work.

items.title: Audit fields (CreatedUtc/UpdatedUtc/By)
items.description: Stamp CreatedUtc, CreatedBy, UpdatedUtc, UpdatedBy centrally by overriding SaveChangesAsync (or using a SaveChanges interceptor) rather than setting them in each action, so no write path can forget them. Read the actor from the current request principal via an injected accessor, and always store UTC (DateTime.UtcNow / DateTimeOffset.UtcNow) to keep records comparable across time zones. Never let the client supply audit values; they are derived server-side from the authenticated identity.
items.applicability: All mutable entities that require a change history or accountability, consistent with the platform's append-only audit posture.
items.example: public override Task<int> SaveChangesAsync(...){ foreach(var e in ChangeTracker.Entries<IAuditable>()){ if(e.State==EntityState.Added){ e.Entity.CreatedUtc=now; e.Entity.CreatedBy=user; } if(e.State==EntityState.Modified){ e.Entity.UpdatedUtc=now; e.Entity.UpdatedBy=user; } } return base.SaveChangesAsync(...); }
items.limitation: Interceptor-based stamping is bypassed by raw SQL (ExecuteUpdate/ExecuteDelete or db.Database.ExecuteSqlAsync); those paths must set audit columns themselves.

items.title: Soft delete with global query filters
items.description: Implement soft delete by setting an IsDeleted flag (plus DeletedUtc/DeletedBy) and registering a global query filter (HasQueryFilter(e => !e.IsDeleted)) so every normal query transparently excludes deleted rows. Intercept Delete operations in SaveChanges to convert EntityState.Deleted into a Modified flag-set, so callers keep using Remove() naturally. This preserves referential history and supports undelete, at the cost of rows that linger in the table.
items.applicability: Entities where regulatory or operational history must survive a delete, or where accidental deletes need to be recoverable.
items.example: modelBuilder.Entity<Project>().HasQueryFilter(p => !p.IsDeleted); // To include deleted in an admin view: _db.Projects.IgnoreQueryFilters().Where(...)
items.limitation: Global filters silently apply to JOINs and required navigations, which can drop parent rows unexpectedly; UNIQUE indexes also still see soft-deleted rows, so make them filtered indexes (WHERE IsDeleted = 0) to allow reuse of a deleted entity's natural key.

items.title: Pagination for large tables
items.description: Page server-side with OrderBy(...).Skip((page-1)*size).Take(size) — never load the whole table and paginate in memory. A deterministic, unique ORDER BY (e.g. by a key column, not just a non-unique date) is mandatory, because OFFSET/FETCH over a non-deterministic order can skip or duplicate rows between pages. Run a separate CountAsync for the total; on very large tables consider keyset (seek) pagination (WHERE Id > @last) since deep OFFSET values force MSSQL to scan and discard all skipped rows.
items.applicability: All Index/list pages over tables that can grow unbounded: imported files, chat messages, knowledge chunks, audit log.
items.limitation: Per the platform rule, list/page projections must select only the columns the view needs into a lightweight record and must never materialize large text columns (KnowledgeItem.Content, ImportedFile.RawText, ChatMessage.Content); a full-entity Take() still drags those columns over the wire. Avoid GroupBy(_ => 1) for the count — use a separate CountAsync().

items.title: Server-side search and filtering
items.description: Build filters by conditionally composing IQueryable (apply each Where only when its parameter is supplied) so a single endpoint serves any combination of criteria and EF Core translates it into one parameterized SQL statement. Parameterized LINQ is inherently SQL-injection-safe; reserve EF.Functions.Like / Contains for text and prefer sargable predicates (column compared directly) so MSSQL can use indexes. For substantial free-text search over large content, prefer MSSQL Full-Text Search (CONTAINS) over LIKE '%term%', which cannot seek an index and forces a scan.
items.applicability: Filter bars and search boxes on list pages; combined status/owner/date/keyword filtering.
items.example: var q = _db.Projects.AsQueryable(); if(!string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(term)) q = q.Where(p=>EF.Functions.Like(p.Name, $"%{term}%")); if(status.HasValue) q = q.Where(p=>p.Status==status);
items.limitation: Leading-wildcard LIKE and wrapping a column in a function (e.g. LOWER(col)) are non-sargable and defeat indexes; rely on the database collation for case-insensitivity rather than ToLower() in the query.

items.title: Export to Excel and PDF safely
items.description: Stream exports rather than building the entire file in memory: page the source query (or use AsAsyncEnumerable) and write rows incrementally to a streaming writer so a large export does not exhaust server memory. Defend against CSV/Excel formula injection by prefixing any cell value that begins with =, +, -, or @ with a single quote, so a crafted record cannot execute when the file is opened. Set an explicit content type and Content-Disposition filename, and run heavy exports off the request thread (queued/background) when they can be slow.
items.applicability: Downloadable reports and data dumps from list pages; any export whose row count or formatting cost is unbounded.
items.example: static string Safe(string v) => v is {Length:>0} && "=+-@".Contains(v[0]) ? "'"+v : v; // and: return File(stream, "application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.spreadsheetml.sheet", "projects.xlsx");
items.limitation: PDF/Excel libraries differ in licensing and memory profile; a fully in-memory generator (and loading huge text columns into the export) will still pressure memory regardless of the streaming response, so project to only the needed columns.

items.title: Import workflows (validation and staging)
items.description: Land uploaded data into staging tables first, validate every row (type, range, referential, duplicate) and record a per-row result, then promote only the valid set into the live tables in a controlled batch — never validate-and-insert row-by-row directly into production data. Return a gap/error report so failures are visible rather than silently dropped, consistent with the platform's no-silent-blind-spots import posture. Use batched bulk inserts (SqlBulkCopy or batched SaveChanges) for throughput, and make promotion idempotent so a retried import does not create duplicates.
items.applicability: ZIP/CSV/spreadsheet ingestion and any bulk data load that must be auditable and recoverable.
items.example: 1) bulk-load -> ImportStagingRow(BatchId, RowNo, RawJson, IsValid, Error); 2) validate -> set IsValid/Error; 3) promote: insert into live WHERE IsValid AND NOT EXISTS(matching key); 4) emit report grouped by Error.
items.limitation: Staging duplicates storage temporarily and adds a promotion step; very large loads should bypass the EF change tracker (SqlBulkCopy) because tracking tens of thousands of entities is slow and memory-heavy.

items.title: Editable templates
items.description: Store reusable templates (document, email, report, prompt) as versioned rows with a stable definition and a separate, rendered output, so editing a template never mutates artifacts already produced from a prior version. Keep template content out of list-view projections — it is a large text column — and load the full body only on the edit/render path. Treat user-authored template text as untrusted: render placeholders through a safe, allow-listed token engine and HTML-encode by default rather than executing raw markup.
items.applicability: Any feature offering customizable, user-maintained templates that are filled and rendered repeatedly.
items.example: Template(Id, Name, Version, Body[large], IsActive); render: replace {{ProjectName}}, {{Date}} from an allow-list, then HtmlEncode unless the field is explicitly marked safe.
items.limitation: Allowing arbitrary expressions or unescaped HTML in templates is a stored-XSS and injection risk; richer logic (conditionals/loops) needs a sandboxed engine, which adds complexity and a new attack surface.

items.title: Dynamic report generation
items.description: Build user-driven reports by composing IQueryable from a metadata definition (selected columns, filters, grouping) over an allow-listed set of fields, so the query stays parameterized and EF Core/MSSQL generates a single safe statement — never concatenate user input into raw SQL. Project aggregates into a flat DTO and let MSSQL do the GROUP BY; run reporting reads with AsNoTracking since results are read-only. For heavy or long-running reports, target a read replica or pre-aggregated/summary tables instead of hammering the transactional tables.
items.applicability: Configurable dashboards, ad-hoc report builders, and any reporting surface where the column/filter set is chosen at runtime.
items.example: var data = await q.AsNoTracking().GroupBy(x=>x.Status).Select(g=>new StatRow(g.Key, g.Count())).ToListAsync(); // map allowed field names -> typed expressions, reject anything not in the allow-list.
items.limitation: Per the platform rule, avoid GroupBy(_ => 1) and group-by-constant aggregate projections — they have caused indefinite SQL Server page hangs; use separate CountAsync calls or group by a real key. Truly dynamic columns can defeat fixed indexes and need careful index planning.

items.title: Optimistic concurrency control
items.description: Guard against lost updates in multi-user CRUD by adding a rowversion concurrency token ([Timestamp] byte[] RowVersion mapped to MSSQL rowversion) so EF Core appends the original value to the UPDATE's WHERE clause; if another user changed the row first, zero rows match and EF throws DbUpdateConcurrencyException. Carry the token through the edit form (hidden field) so the POST compares against the value the user actually saw, not a fresh read. On conflict, reload current database values and let the user merge or retry rather than blindly overwriting.
items.applicability: Any entity edited by more than one user or process concurrently, especially approval and status-transition workflows.
items.example: [Timestamp] public byte[] RowVersion {get;set;} // catch: try{ await _db.SaveChangesAsync(); } catch(DbUpdateConcurrencyException ex){ var cur = await ex.Entries[0].GetDatabaseValuesAsync(); ModelState.AddModelError("","Record was modified by another user."); }
items.limitation: Optimistic concurrency surfaces the conflict only at save time, so it suits low-contention edits; raw-SQL ExecuteUpdate bypasses the token entirely, and a stale hidden token mismatched against a server-side reload can produce false conflicts.
